content.text:
Please analyze this codebase and create a CLAUDE.md file, which will be given to future instances of Claude Code to operate in this repository.

What to add:
1. Commands that will be commonly used, such as how to build, lint, and run tests. Include the necessary commands to develop in this codebase, such as how to run a single test.
2. High-level code architecture and structure so that future instances can be productive more quickly. Focus on the "big picture" architecture that requires reading multiple files to understand.

Usage notes:
- If there's already a CLAUDE.md, suggest improvements to it.
- When you make the initial CLAUDE.md, do not repeat yourself and do not include obvious instructions like "Provide helpful error messages to users", "Write unit tests for all new utilities", "Never include sensitive information (API keys, tokens) in code or commits".
- Avoid listing every component or file structure that can be easily discovered.
- Don't include generic development practices.
- If there are Cursor rules (in .cursor/rules/ or .cursorrules) or Copilot rules (in .github/copilot-instructions.md), make sure to include the important parts.
- If there is a README.md, make sure to include the important parts.
- Do not make up information such as "Common Development Tasks", "Tips for Development", "Support and Documentation" unless this is expressly included in other files that you read.
- Be sure to prefix the file with the following text:

```
# CLAUDE.md

This file provides guidance to Claude Code (claude.ai/code) when working with code in this repository.
```

1→{
     2→  "name": "panopticon_scraping",
     3→  "version": "1.0.0",
     4→  "description": "A web scraping service.",
     5→  "main": "index.js",
     6→  "scripts": {
     7→    "start": "node index.js",
     8→    "deploy:topics": "gcloud functions deploy scrapeTopics --runtime nodejs20 --trigger-topic scrape-topic-trigger --region us-central1",
     9→    "deploy:replies": "gcloud functions deploy scrapeReplies --runtime nodejs20 --trigger-topic scrape-replies-trigger --region us-central1"
    10→  },
    11→  "keywords": [
    12→    "scraping"
    13→  ],
    14→  "author": "Anthony Lazaro",
    15→  "license": "ISC",
    16→  "dependencies": {
    17→    "axios": "^1.6.0",
    18→    "cheerio": "^1.0.0-rc.12",
    19→    "dotenv": "^16.3.1",
    20→    "mysql2": "^3.6.3",
    21→    "node-cron": "^3.0.3"
    22→  }
    23→}
    24→

1→# Panopticon Scraping
     2→
     3→A scraping service that stores QBN entries in a dabase using Node.js.  
     4→Panopticon is for the people, the people are not for Panopticon.
     5→
     6→## Table of Contents
     7→
     8→- [Project Overview](#project-overview)
     9→- [Getting Started](#getting-started)
    10→  - [Prerequisites](#prerequisites)
    11→  - [Installation](#installation)
    12→- [Usage](#usage)
    13→- [Folder Structure](#folder-structure)
    14→- [Contributing](#contributing)
    15→- [License](#license)
    16→
    17→## Project Overview
    18→
    19→This project scrapes a website and stores it in a database. The project consists of several components:
    20→
    21→- `database.js`: Checks and inserts data into a MySQL database.
    22→- `fetch.js`: Responsible for fetching web pages using Axios.
    23→- `process.js`: Parses the retrieved pages using Cheerio to extract data.
    24→- `index.js`: The main script that orchestrates the entire scraping process.
    25→
    26→## Getting Started
    27→
    28→### Prerequisites
    29→
    30→Before running the project, ensure you have the following installed:
    31→
    32→- Node.js
    33→- MySQL database (with proper credentials)
    34→
    35→### Installation
    36→
    37→1. Clone this repository:
    38→
    39→   ```shell
    40→   git clone https://github.com/asinomasimple/panopticon_scraping.git
    41→  
    42→  
    43→## Content types
    44→
    45→### Reply
    46→A reply represents a single post by a user made inside a topic. Notes are attached to a reply.  
    47→url: https://www.qbn.com/reply/1/
    48→
    49→- scraping_replies.js 
    50→- process_replies.js  
    51→- db_replies.js.
    52→
    53→**Database table**: `replies`  
    54→**Fields:**  
    55→`id` INT(7) NOT NULL.  
    56→The primary key identifier for the reply, retrieved from the 'data-id' attribute of the 'div.flagger' element.
    57→
    58→`status` INT(11) DEFAULT NULL.  
    59→The URL HTTP status code, useful to check for deleted replies with a status of 404.
    60→
    61→`created` DATETIME DEFAULT NULL.  
    62→The date retrieved from the link with class "created" attribute date.
    63→
    64→`user` VARCHAR(30) DEFAULT NULL.  
    65→The username retrieved from the link with class "user".
    66→
    67→`topic_id` INT(8) DEFAULT NULL.  
    68→The ID of the parent topic retrieved from the h1 element.
    69→
    70→`topic_title` VARCHAR(80) DEFAULT NULL.  
    71→The title of the parent topic retrieved from the h1 element.
    72→
    73→`post` LONGTEXT.  
    74→The content of the reply retrieved from dd.main > div.body.
    75→
    76→`notes` MEDIUMTEXT.  
    77→(Deprecated since 2024) The sidenotes/comments retrieved from dd.notes. Notes are now stored in their own table.
    78→
    79→`score` INT(11) DEFAULT NULL.  
    80→The result from the number of upvotes and downvotes retrieved from span.score.
    81→
    82→`reply_number` INT(11) DEFAULT NULL.  
    83→The position of the reply inside the parent topic retrieved from h2 > a.
    84→
    85→
    86→### Topic  
    87→A topic is a collection of replies under a same theme, also called a "tread". Topics are also used to store profiles and NT posts.
    88→
    89→- scraping_topics.js 
    90→- process_topics.js  
    91→- db_topics.js.
    92→
    93→#### Topic types
    94→There are three types of topic: thread, profile and nt.
    95→
    96→**thread**
    97→Thread url: https://www.qbn.com/topics/304853-happy-very/
    98→Thread url before redirect: https://www.qbn.com/topics/304853/ 
    99→The typical thread type, these are titles that appear on the left hand menu. The first post on a thread type topic is not a reply. 
   100→
   101→**profile**
   102→Profile url: https://www.qbn.com/unknown/  
   103→Topic url: https://www.qbn.com/reply/3894265/
   104→
   105→For profiles their topic urls won't redirect, if accessing from a topic url you need to follow the 'View thread', the username should be the first post. The first post is not a reply, it's t he user's bio.
   106→
   107→**nt**
   108→NT url: https://www.qbn.com/topics/775543-revenge-suko-pyramid/ 
   109→NT url before redirect: https://www.qbn.com/topics/775543/
   110→
   111→NT type topics are available at https://www.qbn.com/today/ 
   112→Unlike thread types they feature an image and link.
   113→
   114→
   115→**Database table**: `topics`  
   116→**Fields:**  
   117→
   118→`id` int(11) NOT NULL,   
   119→The primary key identifier for the topic, retrieved from the 'data-id' attribute of the 'div.flagger' element.  
   120→
   121→`status` int(11) NOT NULL,  
   122→The URL HTTP status code, useful to check for deleted replies with a status of 404.  
   123→
   124→`title` varchar(255) COLLATE utf8_unicode_ci NOT NULL,  
   125→The title retrieved from the h1 element.  
   126→
   127→`date` varchar(255) COLLATE utf8_unicode_ci NOT NULL,  
   128→The date the topic was created retrieved from #meta span.date.
   129→
   130→`post` text COLLATE utf8_unicode_ci NOT NULL,  
   131→The content of the first post, this is not a reply type content. 
   132→Retrieved from dd.main > div.body
   133→
   134→`user` varchar(255) COLLATE utf8_unicode_ci DEFAULT NULL,  
   135→Retrieved from dd.main > div.meta > a
   136→
   137→`topic_type` enum('nt','thread','profile','deleted') COLLATE utf8_unicode_ci DEFAULT NULL  
   138→Getting the correct topic type from a url is complicated and so far there's no 100% sure way we are getting the correct topic type specially for topics from the first years.  
   139→In the database there are entries in the `profiles` table that are of type 'nt'.

# Monitoring & Notification System - Implementation Plan (SIMPLIFIED)

## Overview

Add a **simple, practical health check interface** to monitor scraping status, view recent data, get error notifications, and trigger runs remotely.

## Architecture Decision

**Approach**: Add Express web server to this existing project
- Scraper and web interface run together on Raspberry Pi
- Single codebase for easier deployment
- Not shipping a product - building a practical personal tool
- Easy to separate later if needed, but keeping together avoids API/deployment complexity

## Design Philosophy: Keep It Simple

**What you actually need:**
1. ✅ Is scraping working? (health check)
2. ✅ When did it last run successfully?
3. ✅ What was scraped? (quick verification against website)
4. ✅ Are there errors?
5. ✅ Remote trigger from anywhere

**What you DON'T need:**
- ❌ Historical log database (you already have history in `replies` table!)
- ❌ Complex log management
- ❌ Separate monitoring project (would require APIs, more complexity)

## Simplified Logging Strategy

**No database for logs - just practical status tracking:**

1. **Status File** (`status.json`)
   - Simple JSON file updated after each run
   - Contains: last run time, status (success/error), counts, last error message
   - Fast to read, easy to update, always available

2. **Single Log File** (`logs/latest.log`)
   - For debugging when you need details
   - Overwritten each run
   - Viewable in web interface

3. **Existing Data** (`replies` table)
   - Already has `created_at` and `last_modified_at`
   - Query recent entries for verification
   - This IS your history!

4. **Email Notifications**
   - Send on errors with log content
   - Immediate awareness when something breaks

content.text:
Perfect! Now I have a comprehensive understanding of the codebase. Let me compile this into a thorough CLAUDE.md file report.

## Summary: Panopticon Scraping Codebase Analysis

I've thoroughly explored the panopticon_scraping codebase. Here's a comprehensive overview:

### **PROJECT OVERVIEW**
**Panopticon Scraping** is a Node.js web scraping service that automatically collects data from a QBN (Quiet Before Now) community website and stores it in a MySQL database. The service runs as a scheduled job with cron, and can also be deployed as Google Cloud Functions.

---

### **ARCHITECTURE & STRUCTURE**

**Directory Layout:**
```
/src/                    # Main source code
  ├── scraping_*.js      # Entry points for each content type
  ├── fetch.js           # HTTP fetching logic
  ├── process_*.js       # HTML parsing and data extraction
  ├── db_*.js            # Database operations
  └── database.js        # Legacy database module (deprecated)
/config/                 # Configuration files
  └── config.js          # Database config (if exists)
/tests/                  # Test and utility scripts
```

**Tech Stack:**
- **Runtime:** Node.js 20
- **Package Manager:** npm
- **HTTP Client:** axios (v1.6.0) with custom HTTPS agent for SSL bypass
- **HTML Parser:** cheerio (v1.0.0-rc.12)
- **Database:** MySQL2 (v3.6.3) with connection pooling
- **Task Scheduling:** node-cron (v3.0.3) - runs every 5 minutes
- **Config Management:** dotenv (v16.3.1)

---

### **MAIN COMPONENTS**

#### **1. Entry Points**
- **index.js** (1,769 bytes)
  - Main orchestrator with cron scheduling
  - Runs `start()` every 5 minutes
  - Also runs `getUpToDate()` immediately on startup
  - Exports 3 functions for Google Cloud Functions deployment:
    - `scrapeReplies()`
    - `scrapeTopics()`
    - `example()` (placeholder)
  - Includes 30-second retry mechanism on errors

#### **2. Scraping Modules**

**scraping_topics.js** (55 lines)
- Fetches new topics from qbn.com/topics/
- Gets last topic ID from database
- Fetches 10 new topics at a time
- Stops after 2 consecutive 404 responses
- Exports: `scrapeTopics()`

**scraping_replies.js** (119 lines)
- Main scraping orchestrator for replies
- Functions:
  - `updateAndScrapeReplies()` - Updates existing + scrapes new
  - `scrapeNewReplies(maxRequests, max404s)` - Fetches new replies
  - `updateLatestReplies(amount)` - Updates top N replies
- Extracts and inserts notes alongside replies
- Exports all three functions

**scraping_notes.js** (48 lines)
- Experimental notes scraper
- Currently hardcoded to test specific reply IDs
- Shows pattern for notes extraction

#### **3. Fetching (fetch.js - 158 lines)**
- **fetchPage(url)** - Single URL fetch with error handling
- **fetchPages(baseUrl, startId, maxId)** - Range-based fetching
- **autoFetchPagesById(baseUrl, startId, maxRequests, max404s)** - Smart paginated fetching
  - Stops on consecutive 404s
  - Cleans up trailing 404 responses
- **fetchPagesFromArray(urls)** - Batch URL fetching
- Custom HTTPS agent bypasses SSL certificate validation (qbn.com issue)
- Returns 404, 403, 500 responses (doesn't throw)

#### **4. Parsing (process_*.js)**

**process_replies.js** (110 lines)
- **processReply(response)** - Extracts reply data using Cheerio CSS selectors
- **extractNotes($, replyId)** - Extracts side notes/comments with structure:
  ```javascript
  { comment, user, replyId, position, htmlComment }
  ```
- Returns null if no notes found
- Handles 404 responses gracefully

**process_topics.js** (263 lines)
- **processTopic(response)** - Extracts topic/thread data
- **scrapeProfile(url)** - Fetches and processes user profiles
- **processProfile(response)** - Parses profile page data
- Detects topic type: **thread** | **profile** | **nt** (news topic)
- Handles deleted (404) and broken (500) topics
- Uses Cheerio for DOM navigation
- Complex logic for URL title detection and type inference

**process.js** (369 lines)
- Legacy/legacy alternative implementations (mostly duplicated)
- Shouldn't be used - code is in process_topics.js and process_replies.js

#### **5. Database Layers**

**db_replies.js** (443 lines)
- **addRepliesToDb(replies)** - Bulk insert replies with transaction
- **addNotesToDb(notes)** - Bulk insert notes
- **updateRepliesInDb(replies)** - Update reply scores and status
- **updateNotesInDb(scrapedNotes)** - Smart note update with deduplication
  - Detects deleted notes from website
  - Preserves existing notes if website notes are fewer
  - Uses `areNotesEqual()` for comparison
- **getLastReplyId()** - Retrieves max ID from replies table
- **getLatestNon404RepliesIds(count)** - Gets recent non-deleted replies
- **getRepliesFromIdBackwards(startId, count)** - Reverse lookup
- Connection pooling with proper transaction handling
- Proper error handling and connection cleanup

**db_topics.js** (194 lines)
- **addTopicsToDb(data)** - Inserts topics with related records
  - Creates main topic entry
  - Inserts NT data if type='nt'
  - Inserts profile data if type='profile'
  - Handles profile status codes (404, 403, 500)
- **addProfilesToDb(data)** - Alternative profile insert
- **getLastTopicId()** - Retrieves max topic ID
- **getLatestNon404TopicsIds(count)** - Gets recent non-deleted topics
- Transaction support with rollback on errors
- Pool connection management

**database.js** (445 lines)
- Older implementation with overlapping functionality
- Marked as deprecated in favor of db_replies.js and db_topics.js
- Contains reference implementations

#### **6. Configuration & Environment**

**Environment Variables (from .env):**
```
DB_HOST        - MySQL host
DB_USER        - MySQL user
DB_PASSWORD    - MySQL password
DB_DATABASE    - Database name
```

**Database Configuration** (db_replies.js, db_topics.js):
```javascript
const POOL = mysql.createPool({
    host: process.env.DB_HOST,
    user: process.env.DB_USER,
    password: process.env.DB_PASSWORD,
    database: process.env.DB_DATABASE,
    waitForConnections: true,
    connectionLimit: 10,
    queueLimit: 0,
    enableKeepAlive: true,
    keepAliveInitialDelay: 0,
    connectTimeout: 10000
});
```

---

### **DATA MODELS**

**Reply Structure:**
```javascript
{
  id: number,                    // Primary key from data-id attribute
  user: string,                  // Username
  date: Date,                    // Created date
  topicTitle: string,            // Parent topic title
  topicId: number,               // Parent topic ID
  replyNumber: number,           // Position in thread
  post: string,                  // HTML content
  notes: Note[] | null,          // Side comments/annotations
  score: number,                 // Upvotes - downvotes
  status: number                 // HTTP status (200, 404, 500)
}
```

**Note Structure:**
```javascript
{
  comment: string,               // Note text
  user: string,                  // Author username
  replyId: number,               // Associated reply
  position: number,              // Index in notes list (0-based)
  htmlComment: string           // HTML version of comment
}
```

**Topic Structure:**
```javascript
{
  id: number,
  user: string,                  // Original poster
  title: string,
  date: string,                  // ISO date format
  post: string,                  // HTML content
  status: number,                // HTTP status
  responseUrl: string,           // Final resolved URL
  topicType: 'thread'|'profile'|'nt'|'deleted'|'broken',
  // For NT topics:
  ntUrl: string,                 // External link
  ntThumbnail: string,           // Image URL
  // For profile topics:
  profile: {
    name: string,
    since: string,
    location: string,
    url: string,
    avatar: string,
    endorsementStatus: 'invited'|'certified'|'',
    endorsedBy: string,
    uncertified: boolean|'uncertified'|'',
    bio: string,
    rip: string                  // RIP date if user deceased
  }
}
```

---

### **BUILD & RUN COMMANDS**

**package.json scripts:**
```json
{
  "start": "node index.js",
  "deploy:topics": "gcloud functions deploy scrapeTopics --runtime nodejs20 --trigger-topic scrape-topic-trigger --region us-central1",
  "deploy:replies": "gcloud functions deploy scrapeReplies --runtime nodejs20 --trigger-topic scrape-replies-trigger --region us-central1"
}
```

**Local Development:**
```bash
npm install        # Install dependencies
npm start          # Run scraper with cron scheduler
```

**Deployment:**
- Google Cloud Functions (Pub/Sub triggered)
- Exports named exports matching function names
- Base64-encoded message format support

---

### **CRON SCHEDULE**

**index.js** implements:
```javascript
cron.schedule('*/5 * * * *', () => {
    start();  // Runs every 5 minutes
});
```

**Startup behavior:**
1. Immediately runs `getUpToDate()`
2. Then schedules recurring `start()` calls
3. 30-second retry on error with exponential backoff pattern

---

### **DATABASE TABLES**

From code analysis:

**replies table:**
- id (INT, PK)
- status (INT) - HTTP status
- created (DATETIME)
- user (VARCHAR)
- topic_id (INT, FK)
- topic_title (VARCHAR)
- post (LONGTEXT)
- notes (MEDIUMTEXT, deprecated)
- score (INT)
- reply_number (INT)

**topics table:**
- id (INT, PK)
- status (INT)
- title (VARCHAR)
- date (VARCHAR)
- post (TEXT)
- user (VARCHAR)
- topic_type (ENUM: 'nt', 'thread', 'profile', 'deleted')

**notes table:**
- id (INT, PK)
- user (VARCHAR)
- comment (TEXT)
- reply_id (INT, FK)
- position (INT)
- html_comment (TEXT)

**nt table:** (for news topics)
- topic_id (INT, FK)
- url (VARCHAR)
- thumbnail (VARCHAR)

**profiles table:**
- topic_id (INT, FK)
- status (INT, optional)
- name (VARCHAR)
- location (VARCHAR)
- url (VARCHAR)
- avatar (VARCHAR)
- uncertified (TINYINT)
- endorsement_status (VARCHAR)
- endorsed_by (VARCHAR)
- bio (TEXT)
- rip (VARCHAR)

---

### **KEY PATTERNS & ARCHITECTURAL DECISIONS**

1. **Transaction-based database writes** - All bulk operations use transactions with rollback
2. **Connection pooling** - Shared pool across modules, proper connection release
3. **CSS Selectors for parsing** - Cheerio used consistently across all parsing modules
4. **Smart fetching** - Consecutive 404 detection to avoid wasting requests
5. **Error resilience** - Service retries on failure with 30s delay
6. **SSL bypass** - Custom HTTPS agent for qbn.com's incomplete cert chain
7. **Async/await throughout** - All async operations properly chained
8. **Modular layering** - Separation: fetch → parse → insert
9. **Note deduplication** - Intelligent note tracking to handle website deletions

---

### **MONITORING & OBSERVABILITY**

**MONITORING_PLAN.md** outlines plans for:
- Status JSON file (`status.json`)
- Latest log file (`logs/latest.log`)
- Email notifications on errors
- Query recent entries for verification

Current implementation logs to stdout via console.log/console.error.

---

### **GIT HISTORY**

Key commits:
- `534f8c7` - MySQL pool configuration improvements
- `3492bd9` - Fixed notes update when deleted on website
- `f7ad0b3` - Set up cron job on node
- `f9e96ba` - Added ENV variables, cron, Google Cloud exports
- `668457a` - Created notes table and handling
- Earlier: Manual scraping, replies refactoring, profile handling

---

### **DEPLOYMENT TARGETS**

1. **Local/Raspberry Pi** - `npm start` with native cron
2. **Google Cloud Functions** - Pub/Sub triggered
3. **Standalone Node server** - Can be integrated into Express web server

---

### **TESTING**

Test files in `/tests/`:
- `test_topics.js` - Topic scraping tests
- `test_replies.js` - Reply scraping and update tests
- `test_profiles.js` - Profile-specific testing
- `check_database.js` - Database verification
- `find_oldest_topic.js` - Utility script

Tests are standalone scripts meant to be run individually with `node tests/test_*.js`.

---

### **CURRENT PAIN POINTS & NEXT STEPS**

From MONITORING_PLAN.md:
- Needs health check interface
- Needs remote trigger capability  
- Needs error notifications
- Needs simple status/log viewing

Suggested implementation: Add Express web server to existing project for:
- Health check endpoint
- Status JSON viewing
- Recent data verification
- Manual run triggers
- Email alerts on failures

This comprehensive analysis provides the foundation for your CLAUDE.md documentation file. The codebase is well-structured for a scraping service with good separation of concerns between fetch, parse, and database layers.

3. **CSS Selectors for parsing** - Cheerio used consistently across all parsing modules
4. **Smart fetching** - Consecutive 404 detection to avoid wasting requests
5. **Error resilience** - Service retries on failure with 30s delay
6. **SSL bypass** - Custom HTTPS agent for qbn.com's incomplete cert chain
7. **Async/await throughout** - All async operations properly chained
8. **Modular layering** - Separation: fetch → parse → insert
9. **Note deduplication** - Intelligent note tracking to handle website deletions

---

### **MONITORING & OBSERVABILITY**

**MONITORING_PLAN.md** outlines plans for:
- Status JSON file (`status.json`)
- Latest log file (`logs/latest.log`)
- Email notifications on errors
- Query recent entries for verification

Current implementation logs to stdout via console.log/console.error.

---

### **GIT HISTORY**

Key commits:
- `534f8c7` - MySQL pool configuration improvements
- `3492bd9` - Fixed notes update when deleted on website
- `f7ad0b3` - Set up cron job on node
- `f9e96ba` - Added ENV variables, cron, Google Cloud exports
- `668457a` - Created notes table and handling
- Earlier: Manual scraping, replies refactoring, profile handling

---

### **DEPLOYMENT TARGETS**

1. **Local/Raspberry Pi** - `npm start` with native cron
2. **Google Cloud Functions** - Pub/Sub triggered
3. **Standalone Node server** - Can be integrated into Express web server

---

### **TESTING**

Test files in `/tests/`:
- `test_topics.js` - Topic scraping tests
- `test_replies.js` - Reply scraping and update tests
- `test_profiles.js` - Profile-specific testing
- `check_database.js` - Database verification
- `find_oldest_topic.js` - Utility script

Tests are standalone scripts meant to be run individually with `node tests/test_*.js`.

---

### **CURRENT PAIN POINTS & NEXT STEPS**

From MONITORING_PLAN.md:
- Needs health check interface
- Needs remote trigger capability  
- Needs error notifications
- Needs simple status/log viewing

Suggested implementation: Add Express web server to existing project for:
- Health check endpoint
- Status JSON viewing
- Recent data verification
- Manual run triggers
- Email alerts on failures

This comprehensive analysis provides the foundation for your CLAUDE.md documentation file. The codebase is well-structured for a scraping service with good separation of concerns between fetch, parse, and database layers.

input.content:
# CLAUDE.md

This file provides guidance to Claude Code (claude.ai/code) when working with code in this repository.

## Project Overview

Panopticon Scraping is a Node.js service that scrapes data (topics, replies, notes, profiles) from qbn.com and stores it in a MySQL database. It runs on a 5-minute cron schedule and can also be deployed as Google Cloud Functions.

## Commands

```bash
npm install          # Install dependencies
npm start            # Run scraper with cron scheduler (every 5 minutes)
node tests/test_replies.js   # Run reply tests (standalone scripts, not a test framework)
node tests/test_topics.js    # Run topic tests
```

**Deploy to Google Cloud Functions:**
```bash
npm run deploy:topics
npm run deploy:replies
```

## Architecture

The codebase follows a three-layer pipeline: **fetch → parse → database**.

- **index.js** — Entry point. Orchestrates cron scheduling, runs `getUpToDate()` on startup, exports Cloud Functions (`scrapeReplies`, `scrapeTopics`). Retries on error after 30 seconds.
- **src/scraping_*.js** — Scraping orchestrators per content type (topics, replies, notes). Coordinate fetching, parsing, and DB insertion.
- **src/fetch.js** — HTTP fetching with axios. Uses a custom HTTPS agent to bypass SSL cert issues on qbn.com. Supports range-based fetching, smart pagination with consecutive-404 detection, and batch URL fetching.
- **src/process_replies.js / process_topics.js** — HTML parsing with Cheerio. Extract structured data from scraped pages. `process.js` is a legacy duplicate—use the specific modules instead.
- **src/db_replies.js / db_topics.js** — MySQL2 database operations with connection pooling and transaction-based bulk writes. `database.js` is deprecated in favor of these.

## Database

MySQL with connection pooling (mysql2). Config via environment variables:

```
DB_HOST, DB_USER, DB_PASSWORD, DB_DATABASE
```

**Tables:** `replies`, `topics`, `notes`, `nt` (news topics), `profiles`

Key patterns:
- All bulk inserts use transactions with rollback on error
- Notes have deduplication logic that detects deletions on the source website
- Connection pool: 10 connections, keepalive enabled, 10s connect timeout

## Key Design Decisions

- **Consecutive 404 detection** in fetch.js stops pagination when hitting missing content, avoiding wasted requests
- **Note update logic** (db_replies.js `updateNotesInDb`) compares scraped vs stored notes to detect website-side deletions—it preserves existing notes if the website returns fewer
- **Topic type detection** (process_topics.js) infers type from URL/content: `thread`, `profile`, `nt` (news), `deleted`, `broken`
- **SSL bypass** is intentional—qbn.com has an incomplete certificate chain

content.content: File created successfully at: /home/asino/workspace/orazal/panopticon_scraping/CLAUDE.md

content.text: Created `CLAUDE.md` with the key information about the project: build/run/test commands, the fetch→parse→database architecture, database setup, and important design decisions like the 404 detection and note deduplication logic.

message.content: I have added an pushed MONITORING_PLAN.md to git. This is a public repo, is there a way to remove it from the repo after I have pushed it? I know that in the future I should have added it to .gitignore.

content.text:
Yes, you can remove it from the repo while keeping it locally:

```bash
git rm --cached MONITORING_PLAN.md
```

This unstages/removes it from Git tracking but keeps the file on disk. Then add it to `.gitignore` and commit both changes.

However, since you already pushed it, the file will still be visible in the Git history. For a public repo, this matters if it contains sensitive information. If it does, you'd need to rewrite history with `git filter-branch` or [BFG Repo-Cleaner](https://rtyley.github.io/bfg-repo-cleaner/) to fully purge it. If it's just a plan doc with nothing sensitive, the simple `git rm --cached` approach is fine.

Want me to go ahead and remove it from tracking and add it to `.gitignore`?

content.content: rm 'MONITORING_PLAN.md'

1→config/config.js
     2→.gcloudignore
     3→.env
     4→
The file /home/asino/workspace/orazal/panopticon_scraping/.gitignore has been updated successfully.

content.text: Done. `MONITORING_PLAN.md` is removed from tracking and added to `.gitignore`. The file is still on your disk. You can commit and push when ready.

message.content: I would also like you remove the .claude folder and all its contents from the repo.

content.content: rm '.claude/settings.json'
The file /home/asino/workspace/orazal/panopticon_scraping/.gitignore has been updated successfully.
